title: Errors and unexpected behavior when starting containers from a systemd unit
body:
### Issue Description

# Issue
Tried to create a simple systemd unit to stop containers, perform backup and re-start containers.
Stopping and performing backup is fine, but when the `podman start --all` command is issued I get all sort of errors regarding cgroups and notify like:

```
Failed to open cgroups file: /sys/fs/cgroup/machine.slice/machine-libpod_pod_xxx.slice/libpod-xxx.scope/container/memory.events
conmon xxx <nwarn>: Failed to add inotify watch for /sys/fs/cgroup/machine.slice/machine-libpod_pod_xxx.slice/libpod-xxx.scope/container/memory.events
...etc
```

After these errors appear, some containers' output gets attached and printed out in the systemd unit, which is something that has not been asked to the `podman start` command (did not specify any flag) and some other containers just fail to start and their state switch to "improper", so they cannot be started or stopped, I just have to `podman rm -f` them.

# Workaround
This might be completely wrong as all podman requirements might not be met and everything else, but after searching a bit on the source code I noticed that podman is setting up something when the `INVOCATION_ID` environment variable is set. Just unsetting it (in a shell script in my case) before running `podman stop` and `podman start` fixes everything.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create a `forking` or `oneshot` systemd unit that contains both `podman stop --all` and `podman start --all` commands.
2. Notice the errors regarding cgroups and inotify
3. Some containers might even start, but their output is then attached to the systemd unit log.
4. After a while the systemd unit is killed, probably for timeout.


### Describe the results you received

I see the aforementioned errors in my systemd unit log and some containers are started, but their output gets attached to the systemd unit log and then the unit gets killed and everything is messy.

### Describe the results you expected

When issuing `podman stop --all` and `podman start --all` from a systemd unit I would expect it to just stop and start all containers.

### podman info output

If you are unable to run podman info for any reason, please provide the podman version, operating system and its version and the architecture you are running.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

The podman version is 5.0.3 and the OS is NixOS 24.05. The SBC is a Raspberry Pi 5 with UEFI.

`podman version`:
```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.0.3
API Version:  5.0.3
Go Version:   go1.22.6
Built:        Tue Jan  1 01:00:00 1980
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
It is generally not recommended to use your own systemd units like that as there are many pitfalls. The recommend things is to have one unit per container viaquadlet or the old `podman generate systemd --new` command. these are known to work and we fix any issues we find there.

Using --all with something like Type=forking makes no sense at all as systemd would be unable to find the right main process of the unit. oneshot should work but you likely need to also use RemainAfterExit=true so systemd doesn't kill the running processes, i.e.
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/systemd/system/podman-restart.service.in
